label: Los Pinos Presidential Residence
sub: Official residence and seat of power of the Fox presidency. Fox governed from here December 1, 2000 to November 30, 2006. Located within Chapultepec forest in Mexico City.

label: Palacio Legislativo de San Lázaro
sub: Chamber of Deputies. Site of Fox's inauguration (Dec 1, 2000), EZLN Comandante Esther's historic speech (Mar 28, 2001), the desafuero vote (Apr 7, 2005), and the legislative brawl preceding Calderón's inauguration (Nov 30, 2006).

label: Zócalo, Mexico City
sub: Central plaza and political focal point. Site of the EZLN Zapatour arrival (Mar 11, 2001), 300,000-person anti-desafuero protest (Apr 2005), and AMLO's protest tent city (Aug–Sep 2006). Mexico's most politically symbolic public space.

label: Fox Ranch 'La Cristalería', San Cristóbal, Guanajuato
sub: Fox's personal ranch and symbolic home. Site of the landmark February 16, 2001 Fox-Bush bilateral summit — Bush's first foreign trip as president — which launched comprehensive immigration negotiations and the 'whole enchilada' proposal.

label: Guanajuato (State Capital)
sub: Fox's home state where he served as governor (1995–1999). His political base and the heartland of the PAN's traditional strength in Mexico's Bajío region. Agricultural and industrial state central to Fox's political identity.

label: Monterrey — UN Financing for Development Conference
sub: Site of the March 2002 UN International Conference on Financing for Development, hosted by Fox. Also the location of the Fox-Castro diplomatic incident, where Fox privately asked Castro to leave early, triggering a severe rupture in Mexico-Cuba relations.

label: Oaxaca City — APPO Occupation 2006
sub: Capital of Oaxaca state. Starting May 2006, teachers' strike escalated into a 150-day APPO occupation of the city center, radio stations, and government buildings. Federal Police entered October 29, 2006. Site of the most serious internal political crisis of Fox's presidency.

label: San Cristóbal de las Casas, Chiapas
sub: Cultural capital of Chiapas and historical center of EZLN-related activity. Starting point of peace negotiations between Fox government and EZLN in early 2001. Fox partially withdrew military checkpoints near here as a good-faith gesture before the Zapatour.

label: La Realidad, Chiapas — EZLN Autonomous Zone
sub: Community in the Lacandon jungle and key EZLN autonomous area. One of the starting points of the Zapatour march in February 2001. After the failed indigenous law, EZLN retreated to its autonomous communities ('caracoles') including areas near La Realidad.

label: Tijuana — US-Mexico Border Crossing
sub: Mexico's busiest border crossing with the US (San Diego). Central to Fox's immigration agenda and the 'whole enchilada' proposal. Post-9/11 border security measures significantly tightened crossing. Increasingly affected by cartel violence from the Arellano Félix (Tijuana cartel) during Fox's presidency.

label: Ciudad Juárez — Binational Metropolis
sub: Largest border city, paired with El Paso, Texas. Key crossing point for migrants and NAFTA trade. Rising cartel violence during Fox's presidency as Sinaloa and Juárez cartels competed for the corridor. Harbinger of the catastrophic violence that would explode under Calderón.

label: Nuevo Laredo — Largest NAFTA Trade Crossing
sub: The busiest commercial border crossing in North America, handling the majority of overland NAFTA trade between Mexico and the US. Under Fox, bilateral trade grew from ~$263bn (2000) to ~$332bn (2006). Post-9/11 'Smart Border' and FAST programs prioritized expedited commercial traffic through crossing points like Nuevo Laredo.

label: San Salvador Atenco, State of Mexico
sub: Flashpoint municipality. In 2001–2002, residents successfully defeated Fox's plan to build a new Mexico City airport on ejidal lands. In May 2006, a police crackdown on flower vendors escalated into the Atenco crisis — 217 arrests, 2 deaths, and documented sexual abuse of female detainees.

label: Guadalajara — PAN Heartland and Business Capital
sub: Capital of Jalisco and Mexico's second city. Traditional stronghold of PAN and the Catholic right. Major center of Fox's political and business support base. Home to many PAN elites and supporters of Fox's centrist governance style.

label: Paseo de la Reforma — AMLO Protest Camp
sub: Mexico City's main commercial and ceremonial boulevard. Site of AMLO's massive protest tent city (August–September 2006) following the disputed election. The weeks-long encampment blocked the city's central artery and caused significant economic disruption, reflecting the depth of post-election polarization.

label: Palacio Nacional, Mexico City
sub: Historic seat of Mexican federal government on the east side of the Zócalo. Administrative center of the Fox administration. Under AMLO's Mexico City government (2000-2006), the Palacio del Gobierno del D.F. operated as a rival political locus across the Zócalo.

label: IMSS Headquarters, Mexico City
sub: Instituto Mexicano del Seguro Social — Mexico's social security and health institute for formal sector workers. Under Fox, complemented by Seguro Popular for the informal sector. IMSS financial reform was another failed reform attempt of the Fox era.

label: Nogales, Sonora — Border Crossing
sub: Major US-Mexico border crossing between Nogales, Sonora and Nogales, Arizona. Key crossing point for both migrants and NAFTA agricultural trade. The Sonora-Arizona corridor became increasingly important for undocumented migration during Fox's presidency as other crossings were fortified post-9/11.

label: Monterrey — Industrial and FDI Hub
sub: Mexico's industrial capital and center of domestic and foreign investment. Beneficiary of NAFTA's expansion of manufacturing. Citigroup's $12.5bn acquisition of Banamex (headquartered here) in 2001 drove the FDI peak of $30.1bn that year.

label: Chihuahua — Maquiladora Belt
sub: Capital of Chihuahua state and center of the maquiladora export manufacturing industry that grew under NAFTA. Fox era trade expansion benefited the northern maquiladora belt but wages remained stagnant, fueling migration pressure. The region's manufacturing employment fluctuated with US economic cycles.

label: Michoacán — First Military Deployment by Calderón
sub: Fox's home region of the Bajío borders Michoacán, but the state became critically important as the first site of Calderón's military drug war deployment (Dec 2006). Under Fox, Michoacán began showing elevated La Familia Michoacana cartel activity that would explode under his successor.
